sentences.text: I'm surmising that it was getting more dangerous and she didn't want me separated from her.
No, but long enough for public school to roll around.
And I'd say summer and a little bit into the fall.
Yes.
[SIGHS] You know, it's funny, my mother took me to the work camp where my dad was.
I remember it as being a pet.
We bent down to talk to him.
[SIGHS] And my dad was so moved to see us, he had a, a kit, you know, like an army kit with food.
And he couldn't eat.
And he gave me the food, and I ate it.
So I don't think I got a total sense of what a difficult position he was in.
I mean, he told us later that they hauled rocks back and forth.
And surely I must have seen, you know, that he wasn't looking like he had before.
But I guess, in my childish way, I didn't really know how bad it was.
I knew it was bad that my father wasn't with me, he couldn't come.
You know, he was held there.
But I don't think I realized it fully.
And my mother tells me a story that I wasn't-- I don't have a recollection of, but maybe I would sense the danger.
We were on a street car, my mother and I.
Mother saw the Nazis or Nyilas, I don't know which ones, coming into the front of the street car.
And my mother got up and got out the back.
And I've always admired her for having the guts to take a chance rather than to be found out to be Jewish and then really be in trouble.
And I think it was after that that she decided that it wasn't safe enough to be around as a Jew.
And with the help of her lover, who was a Christian, I believe, she got false papers for us and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she faked, she left a suicide note.
She left most of her possessions there so that the neighbors wouldn't give us away, in essence.
And we went to another suburb of Hungary, it was called [HUNGARIAN].
And from that time on, I was not allowed to be with other people.
I remember being alone in the apartment.
And to pacify me, mother got me two cats so I'd have something to play with.
And she had hated cats up till then, but she grew to love them.
And these cats were our companions right through, through the war.
But in any case, I could not go out and be with people anymore because the situation was so dangerous.
So that I think I perceived.
You know, I knew that this-- we were now in a really dangerous times.
Probably, she was giving language lessons, because she had to support herself-- private language lessons, nothing public.
No, I think there must have been a very short time between the time I returned from the village to the time that we got this false paper situation.
Probably.
Yes, it was this painter's name.
His name was Szekely.
And I think his first name was Istvan, which means Steven.
I'm not sure about that.
And [SIGHS] we moved to a different apartment than the one where I remember playing with the cats.
And there, the neighbors [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] would question me about why I called him by his first name.
Funny I should forget his first name.
I refused to call him father because I knew he wasn't my father.
And I had certain loyalties, unless this was one of them.
And so when they asked me, why don't you call him father, I just said, well, I just call him by his first name.
Was his wife, yes.
Yes, yes, the cats lived with us.
One was called Olga.
And the other one was called [? bolcs. ?] [?
Bolcs ?] means wise one because she always sat like this.
And we were very, very fond of them.
Previous, previous to going into the basement, I don't remember much.
I only remember two things.
One was that my mother and the neighbors were playing cards one day, and I was there.
And there was this terrible crash, and I was sure that the building was bombed.
And I crawled over to-- mother went under the table and I crawled next to her, just positive that, you know, I wanted to be near her because we were going to die.
And it turned out that the building right next to us was bombed.
And after that, all the neighbors in the various floors moved downstairs.
And they pooled their-- eventually, they pooled all their food.
They divided-- the women slept in one part of this basement and the men slept in the other part of the basement.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at one point, there wasn't any water.
And they dug into the ground.
And when they finally hit water, it was salty water, so that was a big disappointment.
At this time, my mother was taking care of a friend of hers who was from Belgium, her two daughters.
And one of the daughters was a teenager, but one was my age, and so we played.
And as the food got scarce, we played store, selling food and buying food, we made up recipes.
My mother had a cookbook, which she didn't bring with her.
But she said it was very funny that, you know, our idea of how to cook things.
So I spent my time with this little girl.
Yes, yes.
In fact, the reason my mother was taking care of them is because their mother was going to find their father, who was schlepped away someplace on the front.
And I don't know why she thought she could find him.
As it happened, he came back and she didn't.
So that was not a real wise move.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember playing with and eating onion, onion peels, carrot peelings.
I also remember with great horror-- one day somebody who was cooking, who was in charge of the cooking, made a mistake and put petroleum into the lentils and fixing it.
And they made us eat it because there just wasn't enough and we had to eat it.
So obviously it wasn't poison, or we wouldn't be alive.
But it was horrible tasting, it was just horrible tasting.
But there was only so much food.
And so that had to be eaten.
And food was very scarce.
There was a point in time when we stopped going upstairs to the bathroom even.
We lived downstairs but we'd go upstairs to the bathroom.
The last time we went, there were bullets shooting above our heads.
And I said to mother, I'm afraid, I don't want to go, I'm afraid.
She says, no, no, go, go.
And she just insisted.
And I went because I had to.
But after that, we didn't go.
So she knew that that was really dangerous.
And then nobody ventured out of the basement, which is called the bunker.
And then after a while, after we had been there a while, a woman crawled over, over the shooting.
I forgot to mention, we-- this building was next to a railroad embankment so it provided a natural cover.
And the shooting was going on.
First, the Russians were-- or the Nazis were-- later on, Nazis were shooting at the Russians.
Then the Russians used our building, too.
Nazis used our building, Russians used our building-- because of that embankment where they could shoot over that.
And so this woman crawled over through the crossfire of bullets.
And she begged for food because her daughter had been shot in the breast.
They had no food.
And all the people who lived there collectively said that no, they couldn't spare any food.
There was no food to spare.
And my mother said, I'm going to give her my food, you cannot do this.
And indeed, what she did is she gave her portion, probably for that day.
Because by then there was no individual food, it wasn't mine or yours.
Except these girls were given rations by their mother, which I envied so much.
They had goose fat.
And goose, you know, the fat keeps the goose fresh.
And they got to go into that.
And mother never let me have any of it.
And that was never declared as common stock.
And everybody had their own little thing which they would not put into common stock.
But for the most part, everything was common, and so it was a collective decision.
And mother just made that decision, that that was too inhumane.
A woman had risked her life, her daughter was already shot, so she gave up her rations.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
The question was, what did I remember, right?
That's, well, I suppose along with this comes the fact that when the Nazis came and occupied our building, they used one part of the building for their soldiers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I-- I remember distinctly that I heard Nazis talking about throwing babies against the wall.
And I was just sort of-- I was standing, I mean, I know where I was.
It was like a staircase, they were on the staircase, and I was just sort of standing there.
And I told my mother that I had heard that.
And mother said, no, you didn't, you didn't hear that.
And I said, yes, I did, that's what they said.
So for the most part, you know, you asked me was I told, were things explained.
I don't think any of that was done because reality couldn't be looked in the face.
So if you didn't look at it, it didn't exist.
You know, sort of.
Did you know this was happening to the babies because they were Jewish?
Yes, I knew I had to be afraid for my life.
There was a neighbor in that building who made innuendos to my mother that he knew that we were Jewish.
And he kept her frightened for her life.
And I knew about it, and I knew that we needed to be frightened, you know, for our life.
There's something that I forgot to say, but I just want to throw it in here because this idea of not looking reality in the face.
At one point, there was talk about what should be done with me because my parents realized that the Nazis were coming close.
And I was in on this.
I knew that they were talking about sending me to Switzerland.
And I'd say, I don't want to go to Switzerland, I don't want to leave you.
And I'd carry on tremendously.
And as it turned out, for some reason that didn't pan out.
And ever since then, my dad's thing, when I worry about something, he'd say, [SOBBING] it's Switzerland, it's Switzerland, like don't worry about it.
It will never happen.

So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I guess I'm just trying to say that, for the most part, we didn't look reality in-- at least the children were not made to look at reality or to understand it, but we understood it, it instinctively and just by the feeling tone of our parents, I-- I think.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Despite the fact that I knew that this was happening, I also knew that my mother spoke German with-- it was an SS troop that was occupying the place.
My mother was friendly with and spoke German with the captain.
I know that I was given-- through my mother, I was given gifts that were taken from other children.
So I had silver spoons and things with another child's name on it.

And it was strange because [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know if it was my mother's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sense of humanity or what it was, but when the SS troop had to retreat and this captain had to retreat, he was very sick and they were in a bad shape.
And my mother was sorry for him.
So she had a relationship with him that was humane.
It was platonic, but it was very humane.

She saw him as a person, despite the fact that-- now what he told her was, and I don't know if this is true or not, but what he told her was that at the end, at the end, the SS had to, they were forced to enlist, that they did not do it.
You know how the SS is hated because they did it on their own.
But this is what he said.
I don't know if it was true or not, but my mother believed it, I think.
What was your feelings of living so close to the SS men?
I don't remember other then, well, OK.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Other than remembering this about the children, other than identifying with my mother that this is a nice guy and you know he's to be talked with and he gives you things, I know that when we heard planes above, we'd always pray that they'd be Americans and not Germans.
You know, so we saw the Americans as liberators and the Germans as oppressors.
But other than that, I don't think I had any, and I still don't have any, animosity toward individuals.
You know, I have tremendous feelings about what happened, but, but not individuals.
So I don't think I have any feelings that I recall anyway, except fear, fear of being discovered.
So it's nebulous.
This unknown Nazi, or Hitler, oh, we hated Hitler-- OK, but anyway, speaking of having humane feelings.
Anyway, this unknown Nazism was something to be terribly afraid of.
But the person right here who you know, I didn't have that feeling, other than those guys that were throwing the kids, you know.
So I'm sure I was conflicted.
I mean, I can't say anything about that.
I must have been very conflicted to know how I should feel or what I should feel.
What was your reaction, being a very young child and having to live this secret life, that this man was your father and yet he wasn't your father?
How did that affect you as when you were in the basement, in the bunker?
I don't know.
I know how it affected me in later life.
But I don't know how.
I mean, I hate hypocrisy.
I will not stand for hypocrisy.
I go out of my way to say what I am and who I am.
But I don't know how I felt as a child.
OK, that that's an interesting story.
My father was in a work camp.
At one point in time, when the people in the work camp were going to be taken to the concentration camp, the Hungarian guards-- these were not the Nyilas.
They were just the guards guarding these people-- let people go, provided they paid.
My father had nothing to pay with.
And my father was a very meek man and not a courageous man.
The guy in front of him paid, he walked right behind that guy who paid and just walked out.
And he knew where to go because, when we visited my mother, we told him where we were, so he followed us there.
But of course, getting there, he could not be my father, he could not be my mother's husband.
So he was designated as the uncle.
And uncle, being my mother's brother, would mean that they could show some affection toward each other and they could, you know, interact with each other.
And not that, not that anybody could do anything in, in that basement area because there was no intimacy, because the women were here and the men were there.
But at least there could be some comment.
And I remember him kissing her, her kissing him.
So that's how my dad got, got out.
And he was known as professor someone or another.
I forget what he was called.
And he lived there with the men.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because the men and the women were segregated like they were, when the Nazis retreated, and as I said, the Russians came in, the Russians that came in were very young, very-- these Mongolian young guys, maybe about 17 or so.
They were very childlike.
They-- we knew, the rumor was, I don't know where we got the rumor, but we knew if they came in, they would steal and they would rape.
And so we didn't know it the first time, but we knew it after that.
When these guys came in, they came, of course, to the women's quarters.
The men were over there, they couldn't protect the women.
They came in the women's quarters and, and one of the guys eyes fell on this teenage girl that my mother was supposed to protect.
And first, because she had a bracelet, he took the bracelet.
And then he wanted to rape her.
And my mother, in her usual style, spoke up and said, you know, she's just a young girl, leave her alone.
And with that, she called attention to herself.
And my mother and I were in the same bed and the guy was going to rape her.

And mother asked all those women if they would take me into their bed and none of them would, for reasons that they already saw, that if they called attention to themselves, probably they will be the next ones.
So mother told me to go under the covers.
And I'm trying to picture what covers was I under, because he was in her bed, where was I.
But all I know is I was under the covers.
And I kept saying, mother, mother, what is he doing.
And she'd say, and for years I thought she said, he's talking German to me.
And what she said to me when we compared notes later was, he thinks I'm his bride.
So all that time-- this is another closing your eyes to things-- all that time I was under the covers, I did not want to know what was happening.
And this was another evidence of women's inhumanity to women, that you know somebody could have taken me in their bed.
And mother was very upset about that subsequently.

But after that, I don't know when I'd mention this to you, maybe with the pictures, we kept mother from being discovered because every time the Russians came we'd know, they'd be knocking on the door, all kinds of noises.
Mother would go under the covers, I'd get on top with my feet spread out, her head between my feet.
And one time, we just distracted a Russian soldier that came and saw the cat-- oh, Russian, a cat, whatever.
And so they went off.
But the point was that never again was mother going to be found in a position where this could happen to her again.
And I each time was the intermediary, so I was frightened.
I was frightened.
I knew what was going on here.
And so that was a very, very scary kind of thing.
On top of that, these Russians, there's a song that I absolutely do not want to ever hear again, because they would take my cats-- well, that's another story, but I just thought about the cats.
They'd take my cats, put the cats on a phonograph that was going round and round and watch and laugh as the cat is holding on, you know, trying to not be thrown off by the centrifugal force.
But the other thing they did is they liked this song and they played it over and over and over again.
And we got so sick of it, but this was just evidence of the fact that they were very immature and, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what would I call it-- they were regular soldiers, and you know.
Yes, yes, this is now, this is now after the Russians liberated us, but not, not quite yet.
You know, there had to be a fight back and forth, as I said.
The Russians now live in our building.
Now they shoot at the Nazis until the Nazis retreat, until one point in time when it was declared that war is over.
So this was that intermediary time.
Oh, I don't know.
My mother was furious at both my father and Mr.
Szekely because nobody lifted a finger to help her.
So I don't know who decided.
I would think it was my mother who decided that she thought she better fend for herself.
It I guess it was '45.
And if it was '45, then I was eight years old, yeah.
We went back to the original apartment that we lived in.
I haven't figured this out yet, but there were a lot of things that were not there.
I can't figure out how my mother has all the photographs, all the books, all the pictures, some of the pictures I have on the wall here, she brought with her.
I can't figure out how come those were there.
But they were, you know , we have them.
I don't know if they-- she gave them to somebody for safekeeping.
I'm not saying it was there in the apartment.
But we went back to the apartment and we resumed our old life, such as we could.
And we wound up with some of the things that we had.
And I don't know how that happened.
Oh, I'm sure.
I'm sure, but I don't remember it.
I mean, my memories is being in an empty apartment with two cats when we first moved away.
But I don't remember anything.
Yes, yes.
I thought about that, they must have eaten rats and mice, because nobody would give them any food.
Well, my father became manager of the Jewish Distribution Committee, so that was pretty good.
My mother probably went back to teaching languages.
I went back to school.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So things seemed to be as they were before.
I don't remember not being accepted.
Again, as I say, the story I gave you of being on the outside, it could have happened before or after the war.
I was pretty much of a lonely child.
And I don't know if I was discriminated against as a person or as a Jew.
